title: Quick DNA Extraction for Fungal Barcoding (X-Amp)
author: Stephen D Douglas Russell

steps.title: Begin by laying out (96) 0
steps.instruction: Begin by laying out (96) 0.2mL cells on a 96-well PCR plate rack. This can be done in a plate or strip format. Even though it costs more, I prefer to use 8-strips with individual caps as they are easiest to work with. Only a single sample cell needs to be open at a time, individual rows can be removed for closer examination, spin-down at the end of extraction is easier (vs. plates), etc. Label the cells with the Run #, the plate #, and the row number. Above is plate number 2 on run number "ONT07." Plates are filled from right to left. I usually work with two plate racks, one holding the strip I am currently working on and the other holding the remainder of the filled and empty tubes.

steps.title: I prefer a physical printout for...
steps.instruction: I prefer a physical printout for keeping a record of the sample-cell associations. I use the sheet linked below. You could also keep track digitally on a laptop or computer if that is your preference. Extraction Template.xlsx

steps.title: Before starting, I will typically put...
steps.instruction: Before starting, I will typically put the specimens in numerical order, which includes adding my own lab/herbarium code to the specimens. This makes the final digital spreadsheet preparation much easier. Having the samples in order allows the final spreadsheet to be populated by drag-and-drop in Excel and it allows all of the iNat numbers to be copy and pasted (always with some minor modification) directly into the final sample spreadsheet. As an example, I will lay out all of my samples, mark the bags with a lab code (ex - 1 to 96) and enter the same lab code into the iNat Voucher number(s) observational field (Ex - OSF2022-096). I then pull tissue from the samples in that order, filling the cells from right to left across the strip . (Loading right to left allows the flow to go from A -> H of a typical 96-well plate style format.) The bins I utilize for keeping specimens in order and organized during tissue extraction. I pull 8 specimens at a time from the tub on the left to put on the workstation. Once they are completed, they go in the tub on the right. This process takes some time initially, but it will save time with later with spreadsheet preparation. It typically takes me 15-30 minutes to fully populate the final spreadsheet with this prep-work. It would otherwise take several hours. The ultimate methodology you utilize will depend on the types of samples you are processing, the format of the voucher numbers, and the location of the metadata/images.

steps.title: Add a small amount of fungal tissue to each cell
steps.instruction: Add a small amount of fungal tissue to each cell. The amount of tissue should be small enough to easily drop to the bottom of the tube. Less is better than more. If you can physically see the sample in the tube it is probably enough for a successful extraction. Remember, you will be filling the tubes with tissue from right to left. An example of the amount of tissue that should be added to each tube. For dried specimens, I will typically break off a small section of the mushroom with my fingers and place it on a Kimwipe, blank white paper, or on the field data slip in the bag. From there it can be further broken up using tweezers until you spot a piece of the desired size to put in the tube. For fresh specimens, I will try to pull off the desired amount of tissue for each cell directly from the specimen. **Note - I no longer use fresh specimens for this protocol. They are more difficult to work with. I will typically dry all specimens before attempting an extraction protocol.** Both dried and fresh specimens use the same visual amount of tissue. Dried tissue is MUCH easier to work with than fresh tissue in these small tubes, as the fresh tissue tends to stick to unintended places. Between specimens, just wipe the tweezers off quickly with a Kimwipe. No need to flame sterilize or to utilize alcohol. Birds eye view of my typical workstation on a TV tray in the living room. All future steps take place in the lab.

steps.title: Place 15 µL to 25 µL...
steps.instruction: Place 15 µL to 25 µL of X-Amp DNA Reagent IBI Scientific Catalog #IB47441 into each cell that contains tissue. I typically use 20 µL . Using less solution means the overall cost basis per sample will be lower. This process is easiest with a multichannel pipette. One option is to keep a PCR Rack dedicated to an 8-strip of X-Amp for pipetting. Fungal tissue in 15uL of X-Amp.

steps.title: Once all of the strips on...
steps.instruction: Once all of the strips on the plate have X-amp, tap down each strip with your finger to ensure there are no air bubbles in the bottom of the tube underneath the liquid and that the tissue is completely submerged in the fluid. Spin down each strip for 10 seconds in a mini centrifuge. As you pull them out, once again, doublecheck to ensure the tissue in each cell is in the fluid. It is ok if a sample is too large for the amount of fluid and only partially submerged.

steps.title: Heat the tubes with the tissue...
steps.instruction: Heat the tubes with the tissue for 01:00:00 at 80 °C in a thermocycler.

steps.title: Add 100 µL of Molecular Water...
steps.instruction: Add 100 µL of Molecular Water IBI Scientific Catalog #IB42130 to each cell and replace the caps. If the liquid remains really dark, such as in the second tube in the image below, add a second squirt of 100 µL . I utilize two PCR racks with the 8-strips every other row for this process. It makes opening and closing flip-top tubes easier. It is less of a consideration for strip caps. Fungal tissue in X-amp with 100uL of molecular water added.

steps.title: Optional
steps.instruction: Optional: Microwave the samples for 2 minutes. I typically do this as a part of the protocol. In case you are not familiar, there are a number of microwave-only DNA extraction protocols. I look at this final step as a "thermal shock" heating, which disrupts the cell wall more than a standard heat bath. This template is now ready for use in PCR reactions.
